title: Hormuz negotiation realistic offline fixture
subtitle: Before jumping to a verdict, put the few highest-signal observations on the table.
lede: US and Iran are still in active indirect contacts today. What keeps this worth following is not the heat alone, but Crude and LNG price sensitivity.
section_paragraphs: Start with the points that can already stand on the record: US and Iran are still in active indirect contacts today. Hold those steady first, then widen the frame.

Images can restore a sense of scene, but they should support the story instead of replacing the judgment. The strongest visual thread here is: Key images kept for the article: Axios: Blocked page card kept only as a limitation-aware artifact.; MarineTraffic: Offline fixture screenshot for public ship-tracker evidence.; SentDefender: The post says at least 35 C-17 flights moved from CONUS bases to the Middle East and frames it as a....

The thing worth tracking is not the headline heat itself, but who it reaches and which decisions it changes next. The clearest read-through now is Crude and LNG price sensitivity and Shipping and defense headline sensitivity. That is where a topic stops being noise and becomes a variable.

The easiest place to overstate the story is A finalized settlement or ceasefire has already been agreed; A US amphibious group could reach a relevant public watch position within roughly one to two days. At this stage, restraint is part of the quality bar.

The next useful checkpoints are A finalized settlement or ceasefire has already been agreed; A US amphibious group could reach a relevant public watch position within roughly one to two days; Public tracking suggests a US amphibious posture shift, but it still does not confirm intent. Whichever of those gets verified first will move the story forward.
